title: One condition is rarely enough
instruction:
In Chapter 5 you made decisions with one condition at a time. But real decisions are rarely that simple:

- Log in: correct email **and** correct password
- Get a discount: is a student **or** has a coupon
- Block access: is **not** an admin

Logical operators let you combine conditions. `and`, `or`, `not` — three simple words that make your logic dramatically more powerful.

`and` requires **both** sides to be True:
```
True and True  → True
True and False → False
```

Run this. Then change `password_correct` to `False` and see what happens.
successMessage: Both True → logged in. Either one False → denied. and requires every condition to pass. That's exactly how real login systems work.

title: or — at least one wins
instruction:
`or` returns True when **at least one** side is True:

```
True or False  → True
False or True  → True
False or False → False
```

"Give a discount if the user is a student **or** has a coupon."

Write the condition below. `is_student` is False, `has_coupon` is True — the discount should still apply.
starter:
is_student = False
has_coupon = True

# TODO: give discount if student OR has coupon
print("No discount.")

codeChecks.message: Use or: if is_student or has_coupon:
Print a discount message when the condition is met.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: has_coupon was True, so the discount applied — even though is_student was False. or only needs ONE side to be True. The other can be whatever it wants.

title: not — flip it
instruction:
`not` reverses a boolean:

```
not True  → False
not False → True
```

"Show an upgrade prompt if the user is **not** premium."

That reads like natural English — and that is exactly how you write it in Python:

`if not is_premium:`

Write the check below.
starter:
is_premium = False

# TODO: if NOT premium, print "Upgrade to premium!"

codeChecks.message: Use: if not is_premium:
Print: Upgrade to premium!
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: not is_premium reads almost like plain English. This is Python's great strength — conditions can be written to sound like thoughts. 'If not premium...' is what you meant, and that's what you wrote.

title: Combining and, or, not
instruction:
You can mix logical operators in one condition. Use parentheses to control order — just like in math.

`(a and b) or c` — check a AND b first, then OR with c.

Write the access check: grant access if the user is an admin, OR if they are both verified AND 18 or older.
starter:
is_admin = False
is_verified = True
age = 20

# TODO: grant access if admin OR (verified AND age 18+)
print("Access denied.")

codeChecks.message: Use: if is_admin or ...
Combine with: is_verified and age >= 18
Print 'Access granted.' when the conditions pass.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: is_admin was False, but (is_verified AND age >= 18) was True, so access granted. Parentheses make the logic crystal clear. Always add them when mixing and with or.

title: in — is it in the collection?
instruction:
`in` checks whether a value exists inside a sequence (list, string, tuple):

```python
"a" in "cat"          → True
5 in [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]  → True
"x" in "cat"          → False
```

It reads like English: 'if username **in** banned users'.

Write the check below — if `username` is in the `banned_users` list, print a message.
starter:
banned_users = ["spammer", "bot123", "hacker"]
username = "spammer"

# TODO: if username is in banned_users, print "User is banned."
# otherwise print "Welcome!"

codeChecks.message: Use: if username in banned_users:
Print a banned message.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: if username in banned_users — it just reads. Python's in operator is one of those features that makes you happy to be writing Python instead of something else.

title: not in — is it absent?
instruction:
`not in` is the reverse of `in` — True if the value is **not** in the collection.

`5 not in [1, 2, 3]` → True

"Send welcome email if the email address is **not in** our existing users list."

Write the check.
starter:
existing_emails = ["alice@example.com", "bob@example.com"]
new_email = "carol@example.com"

# TODO: if new_email NOT in existing_emails, print a welcome message for new_email

codeChecks.message: Use: if new_email not in existing_emails:
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: not in is much cleaner than not (x in y). Same result, easier to read. User registration systems use exactly this to check if an email already exists.

title: is — checking for None
instruction:
`is` checks identity — whether two variables point to the **exact same object in memory**. Most of the time you don't need this. But there is one very important use case:

**Always use `is` (not `==`) to check for None:**

```python
if result is None:     # correct
if result == None:     # works but wrong style
```

Fix the two None checks below to use `is` and `is not`.
hint:
if result is None:
if result is not None:
starter:
result = None

# BUG: use 'is' for None checks, not ==
if result == None:
    print("No result yet.")

result = 42
if result != None:
    print(f"Got result: {result}")

codeChecks.message: Use: if result is None:
Use: if result is not None:

successMessage: is None not == None. PEP8 (Python's style guide) requires this. It is also slightly more correct — is checks identity, == checks value, and for None you always want identity.

title: Truthy and falsy — Python's shortcut
instruction:
Python treats certain values as False without you having to compare them:

| Falsy (treated as False) | Truthy (treated as True) |
|--------------------------|-------------------------|
| `False`, `0`, `""`, `[]`, `{}`, `None` | Everything else |

So instead of `if name != "":` you write `if name:`

Run this. Notice how Python checks 'is this empty?' without any comparison operator.
successMessage: Empty string → falsy. Zero → falsy. Empty list → falsy. This is Pythonic code — instead of if name != "": you write if name: and Python understands perfectly.

title: Short-circuit — Python stops early
instruction:
Python is lazy (in a good way). When it knows the answer, it stops evaluating:

- `False and anything` — already False, no need to check 'anything'
- `True or anything` — already True, no need to check 'anything'

This is called short-circuit evaluation. It matters because Python will not run code it doesn't need to.

Run this. Notice `risky_check()` never gets called.
successMessage: is_safe was False so Python didn't even bother calling risky_check(). Use this pattern intentionally: check if x is not None and x > 0 — if x is None, the second part never runs.

title: Build an access control system
instruction:
Let's put everything together. No starter code.

Build an access checker with these rules:
- Must be logged in (`is_logged_in = True`)
- Must be either an admin OR both verified AND at least 18
- Must not be in the banned list (`banned = ["baduser"]`)

Create a `username` variable, set up all the flags, and print `"Access granted."` or `"Access denied."`

Combine `and`, `or`, `not in` — you've learned all of these. You've got this.
hint: if is_logged_in and (is_admin or (is_verified and age >= 18)) and username not in banned:

codeChecks.message: Create and check is_logged_in.
Use 'and' to combine conditions.
Use 'or' for the admin/verified check.
Check 'not in banned' to exclude banned users.

successMessage: A complete access control system! and, or, not, in — all working together. This is literally how every login/authorisation system in the world works. Chapter 6 done!
